Title: The PRK/Rubisco shunt strongly influences Arabidopsis seed metabolism and oil accumulation, affecting more than carbon recycling

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: PRK/Rubisco shunt linked to seed glycolysis, pentose phosphate pathway, and fatty acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PRK/Rubisco shunt (noncanonical Calvin-Benson-Bassham-related carbon recycling bypass in green seeds)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: phosphorylates ribulose-5-phosphate to generate ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, the substrate for Rubisco
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: midstream; key control step supplying substrate to Rubisco shunt
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursor pool for Rubisco-mediated generation of glycolytic intermediates that feed fatty acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as a key control point for operation of the PRK/Rubisco shunt; overexpression increased RuBP but did not increase oil, indicating PRK alone is not sufficient as sole rate-limiting step for final oil accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PRK strongly reduces seed oil accumulation (~34% lower total fatty acids in mature embryos), depletes RuBP and downstream glycolytic intermediates, increases pentose phosphate intermediates and starch, and lowers chlorophyll; increased PRK elevates RuBP and some precursors but does not increase oil content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: loss-of-function mutants (prk-1, prk-2); genetic complementation with PRKpro:PRK-YFP; overexpression/extra-copy line (PRKOX); LC-MS metabolite profiling; GC-FID fatty acid quantification; chlorophyll assay; starch assay; TEM; immunoblotting; light-treatment experiments
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in green tissues and developing green seeds; PRKpro:PRK-YFP detectable in developing seeds and seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: developing green seeds/embryos; seedlings; rosette leaves in complemented line
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: light intensity during seed development (normal light vs high light; rosette- or whole-plant shading) strongly affects lipid accumulation in PRK-containing embryos but not in PRK-deficient embryos
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: endogenous PRK promoter (1.9 kb upstream sequence); native/endogenous promoter
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete loss is seedling-lethal and causes pale arrested seedlings; PRK deficiency reduces chlorophyll and perturbs starch/lipid balance; increased PRK alone does not improve oil and may create downstream bottlenecks
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolomics; Illumina whole-genome sequencing of transgene insertion lines
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PRK is essential for seedling establishment and for operation of the PRK/Rubisco shunt in developing green Arabidopsis seeds. 2. PRK loss causes severe depletion of RuBP and glycolytic/fatty-acid precursor metabolites, reducing mature embryo oil by about 34% while increasing starch-related metabolites and starch retention. 3. PRK-dependent support of oil accumulation is light responsive. 4. Increasing PRK abundance increases RuBP and some downstream precursors but is insufficient by itself to raise final seed oil content.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PRK is a useful engineering target to increase precursor supply for oil biosynthesis, but it should likely be stacked with downstream fatty acid/TAG synthesis genes because PRK overexpression alone does not raise oil; complete knockout is not viable
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study genetically validates the PRK/Rubisco shunt in Arabidopsis green seeds; effects on oil exceed prior theoretical estimates, likely due to secondary effects on chlorophyll/photosynthesis, carbon partitioning to starch, and possibly sink strength/resource allocation.
